## damelo
Dámelo lets you share and manage AI assistant sessions (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) with your teams.

        Export and import all your sessions so nothing is missed. You can:
        - Export sessions with detailed summaries
        - List your own sessions
        - Browse sessions shared with your teams
        - Import sessions from teammates
        - Search sessions by repository

        All sessions are stored securely and can be shared with your team members.

        **CRITICAL: All session_data MUST be properly formatted HTML**

        The session_data parameter accepts ONLY valid HTML format. This will be saved as an .html file.

        **Required HTML formatting:**
        - Use proper HTML structure with <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head>, and <body> tags
        - Use semantic HTML5 tags: <header>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <footer>
        - Use heading tags (<h1>, <h2>, <h3>, etc.) for document structure
        - Use <pre><code> blocks for code snippets, with class attribute for syntax highlighting (e.g., <code class="language-python">)
        - Use <ul>/<ol> and <li> for lists
        - Use <strong> and <em> for emphasis
        - Use <a href="url"> for links
        - Include proper CSS styling in <style> tags or inline for better presentation
        - Ensure all tags are properly closed and nested
        - At the end of the page always include a reference to damelo.sh
       
## drive
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

Before executing any write operation (move, delete, create file, create folder), always tell the user exactly what you're about to do and STOP your turn. Do NOT call the tool in the same message. Wait for the user to reply with confirmation before making the call.

When presenting file results to the user, always:
- Show EVERY file — never truncate, summarize, or skip results
- Group by account (personal, work, university)
- Number each file sequentially within its account group (1., 2., 3.)
- Show the count per account
- Format: **file name** — type, size (e.g. **Invoice_2024.pdf** — PDF, 2.4 MB)
- Folders get a trailing / (e.g. **Documents/**)
- Show total count and total size at the end

When presenting file content (drive_read_file):
- Show the full file content — never truncate or summarize
- For CSV (Google Sheets), show the raw CSV data

When moving, deleting, or creating files (drive_update_files):
- action "move": moves files to a destination folder (server-side, no download)
- action "delete": trashes files (recoverable from Google Drive trash)
- action "create": creates new files with optional text content

You can pass either a Google Drive URL or a raw file/folder ID to any tool that accepts IDs.

Read-only tools (list, read, search) automatically try all configured accounts — no account parameter needed.
Write tools (update, create folder) require an explicit account parameter.
## glpi
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

This server connects to a GLPI instance (IT service management) at ti.pulsosalud.com.

Ticket status codes and emojis (use the emoji when presenting status to the user):
  1=New       🆕
  2=Assigned  👤
  3=Planned   📅
  4=Waiting   ⏸️
  5=Solved    ✅
  6=Closed    🔒

When presenting ticket lists:
- Start each row with the time (HH:MM), then "[<emoji>] — <Status>", then " — ", then #id, title, category
- No numbered list; one ticket per line, in the order returned by the tool (tools default to chronological ascending — oldest first)
- Use expand_dropdowns=true when showing data to users (names instead of IDs)
- For search results, field IDs map to: 1=name, 2=id, 7=category, 12=status, 15=date_creation

Example row:
  06:56 [🆕] — Status — #32654 ELIMINAR ATENCION · ANA Prevention
## gmail
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

Before executing any write operation (trash, tag, send, create draft), always tell the user exactly what you're about to do and STOP your turn. Do NOT call the tool in the same message. Wait for the user to reply with confirmation before making the call.

When presenting email results to the user, always:
- Group by account (personal, work, university)
- Number each email sequentially within its account group (1., 2., 3.)
- Show the count per account
- Bold the sender name: **Sender** — Subject

Tag system for organizing emails:
- "important" → starred emails — actionable, urgent, needs attention
- "credentials" → passwords, API keys, server access, login details
- "contacts" → emails from people the user cares about maintaining a relationship with
Custom tags beyond these three are also supported and auto-created on first use.

Auto-sorted emails:
- Emails tagged with "ai/*" labels (e.g. "ai/finance") have already been reviewed and sorted by the AI.
- gmail_search_messages always excludes ai/* emails and returns their counts in "ai_skipped".
- Always show the ai_skipped summary to the user (e.g. "Also sorted: ai/programming (1), ai/finance (3)").
- To see auto-sorted emails, use gmail_get_tagged("ai/finance") — NOT gmail_search_messages.

Search behavior:
- When the user asks to "show emails", "check email", or similar — just call gmail_search_messages immediately. Do not plan, ask clarifying questions, or add extra steps.

Attachments:
- When gmail_read_message downloads attachments, immediately read them using the Read tool — do NOT ask the user first. The "hint" field in each attachment tells you the file path to read.
- Reading is not a write operation — no confirmation needed.
- For binary files (Excel .xlsx, .pptx, etc.), the Read tool won't work. Use Python (openpyxl, pandas) or the appropriate skill to read them instead.
## jira
This server reads from a Jira Cloud instance (configured via JIRA_URL in .env).

All tools are READ-ONLY. No writes, comments, transitions, or edits are exposed.

When the user asks about "tickets", "issues", or "tasks" — use jira_search_issues with JQL.
Jira Cloud requires a bounded JQL query — always include at least one constraint
(project = X, created >= -30d, assignee = currentUser(), status = "Open", etc.)
before any ORDER BY.

All listing tools (jira_search_issues, jira_search_users, jira_list_boards,
jira_list_board_sprints, jira_get_sprint_issues) auto-paginate and return the
full result set as {count, <key>}. There are no limit / start / nextPageToken
flags — scope the query itself if you need to narrow results.

jira_get_issue returns the full ticket in one call: base fields + changelog +
status history + comments + worklog + watchers + attachments + available
transitions. Don't fan out into multiple sub-calls — everything is inlined.

jira_get_project returns project metadata + components + versions + issue count
in one call.

jira_list_metadata returns all fields (system + custom) + statuses + priorities
in one call — use it to resolve custom field IDs or status names before JQL.

When presenting issues, format as:
  [STATUS] KEY-123 Summary (assignee · YYYY-MM-DD)
## music
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

When the user asks to play music — whether "play a song", "play something", "play a calming song", "play something upbeat", etc. — ALWAYS use the user's Liked Songs as the source. Never search Spotify. The flow is:
1. Call spotify_list_tracks("liked") to get their Liked Songs
2. Pick a song that matches what they asked for (e.g. calming, energetic, etc.)
3. Play it with spotify_play

If they say "play something" with no preference, use spotify_play with random=true for a random pick.

Do NOT ask for confirmation before playing. Just play the song immediately and tell the user what's playing.

When presenting track results, format as:
  1. **Track Name** — Artist(s) · Album
     URI: spotify:track:xxx

When presenting playlists, format as:
  1. **Playlist Name** — N tracks · owner
## obsidian
Personal Obsidian KB of math + CS books. Layout:
  raw/<book>/<book>.md          (hub: frontmatter + nav callout + summary)
  raw/<book>/*.pdf              (sources: textbook, solutions, errata, ...)
  raw/<book>/notes.md           (notes INDEX: frontmatter `book: [[X]]`,
                                  body minimal — children link UP to it)
  raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md    (atomic notes: frontmatter
                                  `parent: [[notes]]` only — book is
                                  reached transitively via notes)
  attachments/<book>/*.png|jpg  (screenshots, diagrams)
  wiki/                         (LLM-compiled output — only on explicit
                                  "compile" / "rebuild concepts" requests)

FOUR TOOLS:
  raw_graph()                              full graph of raw/ (ingest layer)
  wiki_graph()                             full graph of wiki/ (compiled
                                           layer); links resolve into raw/
  read(path)                               read a .md file
  write(path, content="",                  write a .md (use content=) or
        image_path=None)                   an image (use image_path=)

IMPORTANT: discover schemas with ToolSearch before first call.

ALWAYS START WITH raw_graph. Don't read blindly. The graph tells you
what exists, node types (hub|notes|note|other_md|pdf|image), the
wikilinks, and which links are ghosts (target_exists: false). All
read/write decisions follow from it. For wiki work (compile, lint, Q&A
against the wiki) also pull wiki_graph — nodes index|concept|book-summary,
with links resolved into raw/ so sources[] provenance shows as real edges.

NOTES MODEL — ATOMIC, ZETTELKASTEN-STYLE:
  - notes.md is an INDEX node. Frontmatter `book: [[X]]`, body minimal
    (a tagline at most). DO NOT pile entries into notes.md.
  - Each thought lives in raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md with frontmatter
    `parent: [[notes]]` ONLY — no `book:` field. The book is reached
    transitively (atomic note → notes → book), no direct edge.

import argparse
import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path

from supaflow.layout import prune_layout
from supaflow.runner import run_graph_models
from supaflow.schema import build_schema, write_schema
from supaflow.store import (
    now_iso,
    project_dir,
    project_key_for_cwd,
    projects_root,
    store_root,
)


def cmd_view() -> int:
    django_path = Path.cwd()
    if not (django_path / "manage.py").exists():
        print(
            f"[supaflow] error: no manage.py in {django_path}. run from your django project root.",
            file=sys.stderr,
        )
        return 1

    key = project_key_for_cwd()
    out_dir = project_dir(key)
    out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    models_json = out_dir / "models.json"
    schema_json = out_dir / "schema.json"
    meta_json = out_dir / "meta.json"
    layout_json = out_dir / "layout.json"
    config_json = out_dir / "config.json"

    print(f"[supaflow] django root: {django_path}")
    print(f"[supaflow] project key: {key}")
    print(f"[supaflow] output dir:  {out_dir}")
    print("[supaflow] running graph_models...")
    rc = run_graph_models(django_path, models_json)
    if rc != 0:
        return rc

    schema = build_schema(models_json)
    write_schema(schema, schema_json)

    meta = {
        "key": key,
        "sourcePath": str(django_path.resolve()),
        "indexedAt": now_iso(),
        "entityCount": len(schema["entities"]),
        "relationCount": len(schema["relations"]),
    }
    meta_json.write_text(
        json.dumps(meta, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False),
        encoding="utf-8",
    )

    n_e = len(schema["entities"])
    n_r = len(schema["relations"])
    print(f"[supaflow] wrote {schema_json} ({n_e} entities, {n_r} relations)")

    if not config_json.exists():
        config_json.write_text(
            json.dumps(
                {
                    "diagram": {
                        "visibleDomains": [d["key"] for d in schema["domains"]],
                        "performanceMode": True,
                    },
                },
                indent=2,
                ensure_ascii=False,
            ),
            encoding="utf-8",
        )
        print(f"[supaflow] wrote {config_json}")

    if layout_json.exists():
        kept, removed = prune_layout(schema, layout_json)
        print(f"[supaflow] layout: kept {kept} positions, pruned {removed} stale")

    return 0


def cmd_list() -> int:
    root = projects_root()
    if not root.exists():
        print(f"[supaflow] no projects yet ({store_root()} is empty)")
        return 0
    found = 0
    for parent in sorted(root.iterdir()):
        if not parent.is_dir():
            continue
        for child in sorted(parent.iterdir()):
            if not child.is_dir():
                continue
            if not (child / "schema.json").exists():
                continue
            key = f"{parent.name}/{child.name}"
            src = ""
            meta_path = child / "meta.json"
            if meta_path.exists():
                try:
                    meta = json.loads(meta_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
                    src = meta.get("sourcePath", "")
                except json.JSONDecodeError:
                    src = ""
            print(f"  {key}\t{src}")
            found += 1
    if found == 0:
        print(f"[supaflow] no projects yet ({root} is empty)")
    return 0


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="supaflow")
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)
    sub.add_parser("view", help="dump schema.json from this Django project into ~/.supaflow/projects/<parent>/<name>/")
    sub.add_parser("list", help="list indexed projects under ~/.supaflow/projects/")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    if args.cmd == "view":
        sys.exit(cmd_view())
    elif args.cmd == "list":
        sys.exit(cmd_list())

I've analyzed the supaflow CLI. Here's how it works and what I'll build.

## How supaflow's CLI works

It's a **uv-managed Python package with a src layout**:

```
cli/
├── pyproject.toml          # package metadata + entry point
├── uv.lock
└── src/supaflow/
    ├── __init__.py         # __version__
    ├── __main__.py         # enables `python -m supaflow`
    └── cli.py              # main() — argparse subcommands
```

The two pieces that make it a global command:

1. **`pyproject.toml` entry point** (`pyproject.toml:8-9`):
   ```toml
   [project.scripts]
   supaflow = "supaflow.cli:main"
   ```
   This says "create a command named `supaflow` that calls `main()` in `supaflow/cli.py`". Build backend is `hatchling`.

2. **`main()`** uses `argparse` with subcommands (`view`, `list`) and `sys.exit()`s with a return code.

It also stores runtime data under `~/.supaflow/` (`store.py:5-6`, `Path.home() / ".supaflow"`) — note that's the *data* store, separate from where the *tool itself* gets installed.

## Answering your three concerns

**uv** — yes. You install it as a uv tool: `uv tool install`. This builds the package into its own isolated virtualenv that uv manages — your CLI's deps never collide with anything else.

**"Installed in ~ so I don't get path errors if I change paths"** — `uv tool install` puts the executable shim in `~\.local\bin` (on PATH) and the isolated env under uv's data dir. There are two modes, and they differ exactly on the path question:

| Mode | Command | Tied to source path? | Updating |
|------|---------|----------------------|----------|
| **Snapshot** | `uv tool install .` | No — copied into uv's own dir. You can move/delete the source. | Re-run `uv tool install --reinstall .` after edits |
| **Editable** | `uv tool install --editable .` | Yes — points back at `src/`. Edits apply instantly, **but moving the source breaks it** | Just edit files; no reinstall (for pure-Python changes) |

So there's a genuine trade-off for your "what if I change paths" worry: **snapshot** survives moving the folder but needs a reinstall to update; **editable** updates live but breaks if you move the source.

## What I'll create

```
clis/new_project/
├── pyproject.toml          # name=new-project, script: new-project = "new_project.cli:main"
├── README.md               # install + update instructions
└── src/new_project/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py
    └── cli.py              # creates an empty CLAUDE.md in cwd
```

The command will create an empty `CLAUDE.md` in the current directory (refusing to overwrite if one already exists, unless `--force`).

Before I write it, one decision affects the install/update story:

content.text:
Yes — for a one-command CLI the src/ layout is overkill. That structure is the "scalable package" shape (multiple modules, `python -m` support, version introspection). You need none of that to create a `CLAUDE.md`.

The minimal structure that still installs cleanly with `uv tool install`:

```
clis/new_project/
├── pyproject.toml      # metadata + entry point
└── new_project.py      # one file: main()
```

What I dropped and why:
- **`src/` dir** — just nesting. A flat module is fine; hatchling auto-detects a top-level module named after the project.
- **`__init__.py`** — only needed to make a *package directory*. A single `.py` file is a module, no init needed.
- **`__main__.py`** — only enables `python -m new_project`. The installed `new-project` command doesn't use it.
- **`README.md`** — optional. I can fold the 3 install/update lines into a comment header in `pyproject.toml` instead, so the whole CLI is 2 files.

The entry point still works the same way: `[project.scripts] new-project = "new_project:main"` → `new_project.py` instead of `new_project/cli.py`.

Could it be even simpler? A single PEP-723 script run with `uv run new_project.py` needs zero pyproject — but then it's *not* an installed global command (you'd have to call it by path), which breaks your "install in ~, no path errors" requirement. So 2 files is the floor for a real installed command.

I'll build the 2-file version (keeping a tiny README since you'd asked for install docs — say the word and I'll inline it into pyproject instead). Proceeding.

# new-project

A tiny CLI that creates an empty `CLAUDE.md` in the current directory.

```
new-project          # creates ./CLAUDE.md (errors if it already exists)
new-project --force  # overwrites an existing ./CLAUDE.md
```

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into its own isolated environment and
puts a `new-project` shim on your PATH (under `~/.local/bin`). The install is a
**snapshot**: the code is copied into uv's data dir, so you can move, rename, or
delete this folder afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/new_project/
uv tool install .
```

If `new-project` isn't found afterward, make uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # adds ~/.local/bin to PATH, then restart the shell
```

## Update

Because it's a snapshot, edits to `new_project.py` here do **not** affect the
installed command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/new_project/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall new-project
```

# setup

A tiny CLI that creates an empty `CLAUDE.md` in the current directory.

```
setup          # creates ./CLAUDE.md (errors if it already exists)
setup --force  # overwrites an existing ./CLAUDE.md
```

The module file is `new_setup.py` (not `setup.py`) to avoid colliding with the
legacy setuptools script name; the installed command is still `setup`.

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into its own isolated environment and
puts a `setup` shim on your PATH (under `~/.local/bin`). The install is a
**snapshot**: the code is copied into uv's data dir, so you can move, rename, or
delete this folder afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/setup/
uv tool install .
```

If `setup` isn't found afterward, make sure uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # adds ~/.local/bin to PATH, then restart the shell
```

## Update

Because it's a snapshot, edits to `new_setup.py` here do **not** affect the
installed command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/setup/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall setup
```

Yes, I understand. Here's exactly what I'll make `newsetup` do when run in a project directory:

**Sequence (each command's output streamed live to the terminal):**

1. `git init` — initialize the repo in cwd
2. `npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes` — scaffolds `frontend/` (the command creates the folder itself)
3. `mkdir backend/`, then inside it: `django-admin startproject config .` → produces `backend/manage.py` and `backend/config/`
4. Write `backend/.gitignore` with the contents I just read
5. Write `CLAUDE.md` at the root with your three project rules

**Decisions I'm making (tell me if any are wrong):**

- **The `.gitignore` content gets embedded into the tool**, not read from `C:\...\anna\ana\db\.gitignore` at runtime. That path is machine-specific and the tool is snapshot-installed, so reading it live would break the moment that file moves. I'll bake the 67 lines in as a constant. (Trade-off: if you change anna's `.gitignore`, `newsetup` won't pick it up unless I re-sync — acceptable for a scaffolder.)

- **`CLAUDE.md` is no longer empty** — it now carries your three rules. This replaces the old empty-file behavior.

- **External tools must be on your PATH**: `git`, `npx` (Node), and `django-admin`. The tool shells out to them, so it uses your system PATH (not uv's isolated venv) — `django-admin` won't be auto-installed by `newsetup`. If it's missing, that step errors with a clear message.

- **Safety guard**: if `frontend/` or `backend/` already exist in the cwd, I'll stop before touching anything rather than clobber. The `--force` flag will only govern overwriting `CLAUDE.md`.

- **Fail-fast**: if any command returns nonzero (e.g. `npx` not found, network down), `newsetup` prints the failure and stops rather than continuing a half-built scaffold.

input.content:
# newsetup

Scaffold a full project: a git repo with a **Next.js `frontend/`** and a
**Django `backend/`**, plus a root `CLAUDE.md` and a `backend/.gitignore`.

```
newsetup myapp   # creates ./myapp/ and scaffolds inside it
newsetup         # scaffolds into the current directory
newsetup --force # overwrite an existing CLAUDE.md
```

## What it does (each command's output is streamed live)

1. `git init`
2. `npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes` → `frontend/`
3. `mkdir backend/` then `django-admin startproject config .` → `backend/manage.py`, `backend/config/`
4. writes `backend/.gitignore`
5. writes `CLAUDE.md` at the root

It stops before touching anything if `frontend/` or `backend/` already exist,
or if `CLAUDE.md` exists and `--force` wasn't passed, and fails fast if any
command returns nonzero.

### Requires on PATH

`git`, `npx` (Node), and `django-admin`. These are invoked as subprocesses
using your system PATH — `newsetup` does not install them.

### Templates

`CLAUDE.md` and `backend/.gitignore` are generated from real files under
`new_setup/templates/`. They are packaged into the wheel and read at runtime
via `importlib.resources`, so the installed command carries its own copy — no
dependency on this source folder's location. To change what gets scaffolded,
edit the template files and reinstall.

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into its own isolated environment and
puts a `newsetup` shim on your PATH (under `~/.local/bin`). The install is a
**snapshot**: code and templates are copied into uv's data dir, so you can move,
rename, or delete this folder afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/
uv tool install .
```

If `newsetup` isn't found afterward, make sure uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # adds ~/.local/bin to PATH, then restart the shell
```

## Update

Snapshot install — edits here (code or templates) don't affect the installed
command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall newsetup
```

content.text:
Good instinct — verb subcommands (your second form, `newsetup new ...`) is the right call. Here's how I'd build it, short:

## Commands
```
newsetup list                                   # show templates + their components
newsetup new --path . --template default --only frontend backend
```

## Decisions

**1. `--path`, not `--name`.** More flexible (`--path .`, `--path test`, or a full path) and required — no implicit cwd default, so you must pass `.` deliberately. Good call.

**2. Three components: `frontend | backend | config`.** `config` = everything that lands in root (CLAUDE.md, root .gitignore — anything in the template's root). `--only` lists exactly which to build; omit it = all three.

**3. Keep `--only` orthogonal — don't auto-drag config into frontend.** Your "if frontend then also config" rule is surprising and hard to remember. Instead make it explicit: `--only frontend config`. Then `--bare` is just sugar for `--only config` — so you don't need a separate `--bare` flag at all. One rule: `--only` = exactly what you list.

**4. Templates become folders with a tiny manifest.** A template isn't just files — frontend/backend need *commands* (npx, django-admin) plus a few overlay files (backend/.gitignore). So:

```
new_setup/templates/<name>/
  template.toml        # per-component shell commands + description
  config/              # → copied to root  (CLAUDE.md, .gitignore)
  backend/             # → copied into backend/  (.gitignore)
  frontend/            # → overlay into frontend/ (usually empty; next makes it)
```
```toml
# template.toml
description = "Next.js + Django"
[frontend] commands = ["npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes"]
[backend]  commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
[config]   # files only, no commands
```

`new` = for each selected component: run its commands, then copy its folder. `list` = read each template.toml and print name + description + components. Adding a template = drop a new folder in; zero code changes.

## So your line becomes
```
newsetup new --path test_project --template default --only frontend
```

Sound right? If yes I'll: uninstall the current `newsetup`, refactor to `new`/`list` + the manifest loader, migrate the existing scaffold into `templates/default/`, and reinstall.

content.text:
Confirmed. Here's the model I'll build:

**`config/` is not a module — it's always-on root files.**
- Anything in the template's `config/` folder → copied to project root (CLAUDE.md, .gitignore, …), **always**, on every scaffold.
- `--bare` is the *only* way to get config-only (skip all modules).

**Modules are auto-discovered folders.**
- Every folder in the template *except* `config/` is a module: `frontend/`, `backend/`, and any new one you drop in like `migrate/` or `infra/` automatically becomes a selectable module — no code change.
- They show up in `newsetup list`.

**`--only` selects modules; config rides along.**
- `--only frontend` → frontend module **+ config** (root files).
- `--only frontend backend` → both modules + config.
- no `--only` → all modules + config.
- `--bare` → config only, no modules.

**A module = optional commands + its folder contents.**
- `template.toml` declares each module's shell commands (e.g. `npx create-next-app`, `django-admin startproject`).
- A folder with no commands entry is just file-copy (e.g. an `infra/` of static files). `backend/`'s folder holds its `.gitignore` overlay.

So:
```
newsetup list
newsetup new --path test_project --template default --only frontend
newsetup new --path . --template default --bare
```

One thing to nail down before I build:

You wanted to clarify — here's the concrete file structure so we're looking at the same thing.

## The CLI package
```
clis/new_setup/
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
└── new_setup/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── cli.py                    # argparse: `new` + `list`, template loader
    └── templates/
        └── default/              # ← one template = one folder
            ├── template.toml     # description + per-module commands
            ├── config/           # always-on → copied to project ROOT
            │   ├── CLAUDE.md
            │   └── .gitignore        (optional root .gitignore)
            ├── frontend/         # module "frontend" (auto-discovered)
            │   └── .gitkeep          (empty; create-next-app fills it)
            └── backend/          # module "backend" (auto-discovered)
                └── .gitignore        # overlay copied into ./backend/
```

Drop a new folder like `infra/` next to `frontend/`/`backend/` and it instantly becomes a module — shows in `list`, selectable via `--only infra`.

## `template.toml`
```toml
description = "Next.js + Django"

[frontend]
commands = ["npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes"]

[backend]
commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]

# a module with no [section] = just copy its folder, no commands (e.g. infra/)
```

## What `newsetup new --path test_project --template default --only frontend` produces
```
test_project/
├── .git/                  # git init (always)
├── CLAUDE.md              # from config/  (always-on)
├── .gitignore             # from config/  (if present)
└── frontend/              # frontend module: ran create-next-app + overlaid frontend/*
```
`backend/` is absent because it wasn't in `--only`. With no `--only`, both modules build. With `--bare`, only `.git` + the config/ root files.

The one open question is **where each module's commands run** — e.g. `django-admin startproject config .` must run *inside* `./backend/`, while `npx create-next-app frontend` runs at root. Two ways:

**A. Convention:** module `backend` → commands run in `./backend/`, files copy into `./backend/`. Simple 1:1. (Frontend is the odd one: create-next-app makes `./frontend` itself, so its command runs at root.)

**B. Explicit `cwd` in toml:** each module declares its working dir. More flexible, slightly more verbose.

Which do you want — convention (A) or explicit cwd (B)?

content.text:
The issue: each module has shell commands. **Which folder are they run from?** It matters because the two example commands behave oppositely:

- `django-admin startproject config .` — the `.` means "put the project *here*". So it must run **inside `./backend/`**, and `./backend/` must already exist.
- `npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes` — this command **creates `./frontend/` itself**. So it must run at the **project root**, not inside `./frontend/` (otherwise you'd get `./frontend/frontend/`).

So one module wants to run *inside its folder*, the other wants to run *at root*. The CLI needs a rule for that.

---

**Option A — Convention (the rule is fixed in code):**
Module named `backend` → CLI makes `./backend/`, runs its commands inside it, copies `templates/default/backend/*` into it.

```
[backend]  commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
```
→ CLI does: `mkdir backend` → run command in `./backend/` ✅

But `frontend` breaks the rule, because create-next-app makes its own folder. You'd have to special-case it, e.g. write the command as `cd .. && npx create-next-app frontend` or hardcode "frontend runs at root." Ugly. **Convention works until a command doesn't follow the convention** — and create-next-app doesn't.

---

**Option B — Explicit `cwd` per module in the toml:**
Each module just says where its commands run. No hidden rule, no special-casing.

```toml
[frontend]
cwd = "."                                              # run at root; the command makes ./frontend
commands = ["npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes"]

[backend]
cwd = "backend"                                        # CLI makes ./backend, runs command inside it
commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
```

CLI logic: if `cwd` is set, `mkdir -p <cwd>` and run commands there; default `cwd = "."`. File copying is unchanged (a module's folder contents always copy into `./<module>/`).

---

**My recommendation: B.** It's one extra line per module, and it cleanly handles the fact that "create-its-own-folder" vs "fill-this-folder" commands genuinely differ. Convention (A) looks simpler but forces an exception for the very first two modules you have.

Want B?

import argparse
import subprocess
import sys
from importlib.resources import files
from pathlib import Path


def template(name: str) -> str:
    return (files("new_setup") / "templates" / name).read_text(encoding="utf-8")


def fail(msg: str) -> None:
    print(f"[newsetup] error: {msg}", file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)


def run(cmd: str, cwd: Path) -> None:
    print(f"\n[newsetup] $ {cmd}  (in {cwd})")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, cwd=str(cwd), shell=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        fail(f"command failed ({result.returncode}): {cmd}")


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog="newsetup",
        description="Scaffold a git repo with a Next.js frontend/ and a Django backend/.",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "name",
        nargs="?",
        help="project folder to create and scaffold into; omit to use the current directory",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "-f",
        "--force",
        action="store_true",
        help="overwrite CLAUDE.md if it already exists",
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    if args.name:
        target = Path.cwd() / args.name
        if target.exists() and any(target.iterdir()):
            fail(f"{target} already exists and is not empty.")
        target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    else:
        target = Path.cwd()

    if (target / "frontend").exists():
        fail(f"{target / 'frontend'} already exists. refusing to clobber.")
    if (target / "backend").exists():
        fail(f"{target / 'backend'} already exists. refusing to clobber.")

    claude_md = target / "CLAUDE.md"
    if claude_md.exists() and not args.force:
        fail(f"{claude_md} already exists. use --force to overwrite.")

    gitignore_text = template("backend.gitignore")
    claude_text = template("CLAUDE.md")

    print(f"[newsetup] scaffolding into {target}")

    run("git init", target)
    run("npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes", target)

    backend = target / "backend"
    backend.mkdir()
    run("django-admin startproject config .", backend)

    (backend / ".gitignore").write_text(gitignore_text, encoding="utf-8")
    print(f"[newsetup] wrote {backend / '.gitignore'}")

    claude_md.write_text(claude_text, encoding="utf-8")
    print(f"[newsetup] wrote {claude_md}")

    print(f"\n[newsetup] done. project ready at {target}")

input.content:
# newsetup

Scaffold projects from **templates**. A template bundles always-on root files
(`config/`) plus any number of optional **modules** (each its own folder), where
a module can run shell commands (e.g. `create-next-app`, `django-admin
startproject`) and/or drop in files.

```
newsetup list                                          # show templates + their modules
newsetup new --path .          --template default      # scaffold all modules + config here
newsetup new --path myapp      --only frontend         # one module (+ config) into ./myapp
newsetup new --path myapp      --only frontend backend # several modules (+ config)
newsetup new --path .          --bare                  # config root files only, no modules
newsetup new --path . --bare --force                   # overwrite existing root files
```

## Model

- **`--path`** is required — no implicit current-directory default. Use `.` for
  here, a name for `./<name>`, or a full path. The folder is created if missing.
- **`config/`** = always-on. Everything in a template's `config/` folder is
  copied to the project **root** on every scaffold (e.g. `CLAUDE.md`). It is not
  a selectable module.
- **Modules** = every other folder in the template (and/or every section in
  `template.toml`). Drop a new folder like `infra/` into a template and it
  becomes a module automatically — it shows in `list` and is selectable with
  `--only infra`. No code change needed.
- **`--only A B`** builds exactly modules A and B; config still rides along.
  Omit `--only` to build all modules. `--only` and `--bare` are mutually
  exclusive.
- **`--bare`** builds config root files only (no modules).
- **`--force`** overwrites existing root config files. Module folders that
  already exist and are non-empty always abort (never clobbered).
- `git init` runs on every scaffold. If any command exits nonzero, `newsetup`
  stops.

### Requires on PATH

`git`, plus whatever a selected module's commands call — `npx` (Node) for
`frontend`, `django-admin` for `backend`. These run as subprocesses via your
system PATH; `newsetup` does not install them.

## Templates

```
new_setup/templates/<name>/
├── template.toml     # description + per-module { cwd, commands }
├── config/           # → copied to project ROOT (always)
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── backend/          # module "backend": files overlaid into ./backend/
│   └── .gitignore
└── frontend/         # (optional) overlay files for ./frontend/
```

`template.toml`:

```toml
description = "Next.js frontend + Django backend"

[frontend]
cwd = "."                                              # run at root; the command makes ./frontend
commands = ["npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes"]

[backend]
cwd = "backend"                                        # newsetup makes ./backend, runs the command inside
commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
```

- A module's `commands` run in `cwd` (relative to the project root; default
  `"."`). `newsetup` creates `cwd` first if it isn't `"."`.
- A module's folder contents (if any) are copied into `./<module>/` after its
  commands run.
- A module with no `template.toml` section is pure file-copy (no commands).

Templates ship inside the package and are read at runtime via the installed
package's own files — no dependency on this source folder's location.

**Add a template:** create a new folder under `new_setup/templates/`, then
reinstall.

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into an isolated environment and puts a
`newsetup` shim on your PATH (`~/.local/bin`). Snapshot install: code + templates
are copied into uv's data dir, so you can move/rename/delete this folder
afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/
uv tool install .
```

If `newsetup` isn't found, ensure uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # then restart the shell
```

## Update

Snapshot install — edits here (code or templates) don't affect the installed
command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall newsetup
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# newsetup

Scaffold a full project: a git repo with a **Next.js `frontend/`** and a
**Django `backend/`**, plus a root `CLAUDE.md` and a `backend/.gitignore`.

```
newsetup myapp   # creates ./myapp/ and scaffolds inside it
newsetup         # scaffolds into the current directory
newsetup --force # overwrite an existing CLAUDE.md
```

## What it does (each command's output is streamed live)

1. `git init`
2. `npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes` → `frontend/`
3. `mkdir backend/` then `django-admin startproject config .` → `backend/manage.py`, `backend/config/`
4. writes `backend/.gitignore`
5. writes `CLAUDE.md` at the root

It stops before touching anything if `frontend/` or `backend/` already exist,
or if `CLAUDE.md` exists and `--force` wasn't passed, and fails fast if any
command returns nonzero.

### Requires on PATH

`git`, `npx` (Node), and `django-admin`. These are invoked as subprocesses
using your system PATH — `newsetup` does not install them.

### Templates

`CLAUDE.md` and `backend/.gitignore` are generated from real files under
`new_setup/templates/`. They are packaged into the wheel and read at runtime
via `importlib.resources`, so the installed command carries its own copy — no
dependency on this source folder's location. To change what gets scaffolded,
edit the template files and reinstall.

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into its own isolated environment and
puts a `newsetup` shim on your PATH (under `~/.local/bin`). The install is a
**snapshot**: code and templates are copied into uv's data dir, so you can move,
rename, or delete this folder afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/
uv tool install .
```

If `newsetup` isn't found afterward, make sure uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # adds ~/.local/bin to PATH, then restart the shell
```

## Update

Snapshot install — edits here (code or templates) don't affect the installed
command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall newsetup
```

**1. No implicit cwd default.** `newsetup` now requires a subcommand, and `new` requires `--path`. To target the current folder you pass `--path .` deliberately. Bare `newsetup` errors (exit 2). Old install uninstalled, fresh `0.2.0` installed.

**3. Template + module system** (your design, Option B):

new_setup/templates/default/

├── config/CLAUDE.md     # always-on → project root
├── backend/.gitignore   # module "backend" overlay
└── (frontend = toml-only module, create-next-app makes its folder)
```

The model exactly as we agreed:
- **`config/` is not a module** — always copied to root (unless `--bare`).
- **Modules are auto-discovered** — any folder besides `config/`, plus any `template.toml` section. Drop in `infra/` and it's instantly a module in `list` and `--only`.

| full (all modules) | ✅ git + Next.js frontend + Django backend + CLAUDE.md |

# newsetup

Scaffold projects from **templates**. A template bundles always-on root files
(`config/`) plus any number of optional **modules** (each its own folder), where
a module can run shell commands (e.g. `create-next-app`, `django-admin
startproject`) and/or drop in files.

```

newsetup new --path .          --template default      # scaffold all modules + config here
newsetup new --path myapp      --only frontend         # one module (+ config) into ./myapp

newsetup new --path .          --bare                  # config root files only, no modules
newsetup new --path . --bare --force                   # overwrite existing root files
```

- **`--path`** is required — no implicit current-directory default. Use `.` for
  here, a name for `./<name>`, or a full path. The folder is created if missing.
- **`config/`** = always-on. Everything in a template's `config/` folder is
  copied to the project **root** on every scaffold (e.g. `CLAUDE.md`). It is not
  a selectable module.
- **Modules** = every other folder in the template (and/or every section in
  `template.toml`). Drop a new folder like `infra/` into a template and it
  becomes a module automatically — it shows in `list` and is selectable with
  `--only infra`. No code change needed.
- **`--only A B`** builds exactly modules A and B; config still rides along.
  Omit `--only` to build all modules. `--only` and `--bare` are mutually
  exclusive.
- **`--bare`** builds config root files only (no modules).
- **`--force`** overwrites existing root config files. Module folders that
  already exist and are non-empty always abort (never clobbered).
- `git init` runs on every scaffold. If any command exits nonzero, `newsetup`
  stops.

### Requires on PATH

`git`, plus whatever a selected module's commands call — `npx` (Node) for
`frontend`, `django-admin` for `backend`. These run as subprocesses via your
system PATH; `newsetup` does not install them.

```
new_setup/templates/<name>/
├── template.toml     # description + per-module { cwd, commands }
├── config/           # → copied to project ROOT (always)
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── backend/          # module "backend": files overlaid into ./backend/
│   └── .gitignore
└── frontend/         # (optional) overlay files for ./frontend/

[backend]
cwd = "backend"                                        # newsetup makes ./backend, runs the command inside
commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
```

  `"."`). `newsetup` creates `cwd` first if it isn't `"."`.
- A module's folder contents (if any) are copied into `./<module>/` after its
  commands run.
- A module with no `template.toml` section is pure file-copy (no commands).

Templates ship inside the package and are read at runtime via the installed
package's own files — no dependency on this source folder's location.

**Add a template:** create a new folder under `new_setup/templates/`, then
reinstall.

are copied into uv's data dir, so you can move/rename/delete this folder
afterward without breaking the command.

```sh

```sh

newsetup new --template default --path myapp --only frontend backend
newsetup new --template default --path . --bare                  # config root files only, no modules
newsetup new --template default --path . --bare --force          # overwrite existing root files
```

## Model

- **`--template`** is required — you always state what you're scaffolding. See
  `newsetup list` for available names.
- **`--path`** is required — no implicit current-directory default. Use `.` for
  here, a name for `./<name>`, or a full path. The folder is created if missing.
- **`config/`** = always-on. Everything in a template's `config/` folder is
  copied to the project **root** on every scaffold (e.g. `CLAUDE.md`). It is not
  a selectable module.
- **Modules** = every other folder in the template (and/or every section in
  `template.toml`). Drop a new folder like `infra/` into a template and it
  becomes a module automatically — it shows in `list` and is selectable with
  `--only infra`. No code change needed.
- **`--only A B`** builds exactly modules A and B; config still rides along.
  Omit `--only` to build all modules. `--only` and `--bare` are mutually
  exclusive.
- **`--bare`** builds config root files only (no modules).
- **`--force`** overwrites existing root config files. Module folders that
  already exist and are non-empty always abort (never clobbered).
- `git init` runs on every scaffold. If any command exits nonzero, `newsetup`
  stops.

### Requires on PATH

`git`, plus whatever a selected module's commands call — `npx` (Node) for
`frontend`, `django-admin` for `backend`. These run as subprocesses via your
system PATH; `newsetup` does not install them.

## Templates

```
new_setup/templates/<name>/
├── template.toml     # description + per-module { cwd, commands }
├── config/           # → copied to project ROOT (always)
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── backend/          # module "backend": files overlaid into ./backend/
│   └── .gitignore
└── frontend/         # (optional) overlay files for ./frontend/
```

`template.toml`:

```toml
description = "Next.js frontend + Django backend"

[frontend]
cwd = "."                                              # run at root; the command makes ./frontend
commands = ["npx create-next-app@latest frontend --yes"]

[backend]
cwd = "backend"                                        # newsetup makes ./backend, runs the command inside
commands = ["django-admin startproject config ."]
```

- A module's `commands` run in `cwd` (relative to the project root; default
  `"."`). `newsetup` creates `cwd` first if it isn't `"."`.
- A module's folder contents (if any) are copied into `./<module>/` after its
  commands run.
- A module with no `template.toml` section is pure file-copy (no commands).

Templates ship inside the package and are read at runtime via the installed
package's own files — no dependency on this source folder's location.

**Add a template:** create a new folder under `new_setup/templates/`, then
reinstall.

## Install

Installed as a **uv tool** — uv builds it into an isolated environment and puts a
`newsetup` shim on your PATH (`~/.local/bin`). Snapshot install: code + templates
are copied into uv's data dir, so you can move/rename/delete this folder
afterward without breaking the command.

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/
uv tool install .
```

If `newsetup` isn't found, ensure uv's bin dir is on PATH:

```sh
uv tool update-shell   # then restart the shell
```

## Update

Snapshot install — edits here (code or templates) don't affect the installed
command until you reinstall:

```sh
# from clis/new_setup/, after editing
uv tool install --reinstall .
```

## Uninstall

```sh
uv tool uninstall newsetup
```

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\franc\.claude\skills\git-push

# git-push

Safely commit and push the current changes. Every run follows this exact sequence:

## 1. Safety check

Run `git status` to see what will be staged. Review the actual file list for:

- **Secrets** — `.env`, `.pem`, `.key`, credentials, tokens, API keys
- **Internal data** — data dumps, migration exports, board exports, JSON/CSV with names/tasks/internal info, database exports. Any folder that looks like a one-off dump (e.g. `*-migration/`, `*-export/`, `*-dump/`) is a red flag.
- **Large binaries** — blobs, images, compiled files that don't belong in git
- **Files that should be gitignored** — check if the repo is public, and whether the file contains anything that shouldn't be on GitHub
- **Local filesystem paths** — grep staged text files (README, docs, configs, examples) for absolute local paths: `C:\Users\...`, `C:/Francisco/...`, `/Users/<name>/...`, `/home/<name>/...`, `/mnt/c/...`, `~/<name>/...`. These leak the author's machine layout and username. Also watch for hardcoded local hostnames, LAN IPs (`192.168.*`, `10.*`), and `localhost:<port>` references that only make sense on the author's box.

Local paths are a **fixable cosmetic leak**, not a blocker. When you find one:

1. Replace it with a generic placeholder (`/path/to/repo`, `<repo-root>`, `~/projects/<repo>`) or a repo-relative path.
2. If the path is inside an example command, keep the command runnable — use a clearly-placeholder token like `<your-username>` so readers know to substitute.
3. Re-stage the edited file before committing. Do NOT ask the user for permission to scrub a local path — just fix it and mention it in the heads-up.

Only stop if you find a **real problem** (secrets, dumps, binaries). Do NOT theorize about what .gitignore *could* be missing — check the actual files. If every file in the diff is safe, move on silently.

## 2. Initialize git if missing

If `.git/` does not exist, run `git init` and continue. If it exists, skip.

## 3. Stage everything

```bash
git add .
```

## 4. Write the commit message

Use **conventional commits**. No co-authoring.

Every message MUST answer both **what** changed and **why** it changed.

- `feat:` for new functionality
- `fix:` for bug fixes
- `chore:` for everything else

Bad: `feat: add repo mapping` — missing the why.
Good: `feat: add repo mapping to centralize project navigation`

Bad: `fix: update query logic` — what query? why?
Good: `fix: deduplicate subsidiary query to avoid double-counting schedules`

Bad: `chore: update dependencies` — why now?
Good: `chore: bump django to 4.2.9 to patch CVE-2024-XXXXX`

Before writing the message, inspect both:

- the staged diff
- the current chat context / recent user requests

Check whether the staged work represents **one logical change** or **multiple logical changes bundled together** because everything was staged with `git add .`.

If the diff includes multiple distinct tasks or features, the commit message must say so explicitly instead of pretending everything is one small change. In that case:

- write a multi-line conventional commit message
- use one `type:` line per logical change
- make each line include both **what** changed and **why**
- order the lines by importance, with the main user-facing change first

Bad multi-change message:
`feat: update spotify playback`

Good multi-change message:
`feat: return playlist context in spotify playback responses to show what started playing
feat: add Droid MCP and skill deployment guidance to document the new local setup`

Another good multi-change message:
`feat: deploy personal skills to Droid to keep Claude and Droid installs in sync
docs: update skill guidelines to point at deploy_skills.py and Droid skill folders`

Do NOT invent a fake single-feature message when the diff clearly contains unrelated or semi-related work. The message should reflect the true scope of the commit.

Read the diff to understand the changes, then write the message. Use a HEREDOC:

```bash
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
type: what changed and why
EOF
)"
```

If there is nothing to commit yet (empty repo), create an initial commit after staging so step 5's `gh repo create --push` has something to push.

## 5. Ensure a GitHub remote exists

Run `git remote -v`. Three cases:

**a) A remote is already set** → skip to step 6.

**b) No remote at all** → create one on GitHub using `gh`:

1. Verify auth: `gh auth status`. If not logged in, stop and ask the user to run `gh auth login`.
2. Pick a repo name. Default: the current directory's basename. Confirm with the user before creating.
3. Default to **private** unless the user explicitly says public. Creating a public repo without asking is a mistake — code can contain secrets, half-finished work, or internal context the user didn't mean to publish.
4. Create and push in one call:

   ```bash
   gh repo create <name> --source=. --private --push
   ```

   This creates the GitHub repo, wires up `origin`, and pushes the current branch with upstream tracking. Do not run `git push` again afterward.

**c) Remote exists but the current branch has no upstream** → push with `-u`:

```bash
git push -u origin <current-branch>
```

## 6. Push

If step 5 already pushed (cases b and c), skip this step. Otherwise:

```bash
git push
```

**Read the push output.** If GitHub has renamed or transferred the repo, it prints something like:

```
remote: This repository moved. Please use the new location:
remote:   https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

The push still succeeds via redirect, but the local `origin` still points at the old name. When you see this notice, update the URL on the spot:

```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

Use the exact URL printed after "new location:". Tell the user you updated the origin URL and show the new value.

## Rules

- NEVER skip any step.
- NEVER push if the audit flagged something — resolve it first.
- NEVER add a Co-Authored-By line.
- NEVER create a public repo without explicit user consent. Default to private.
- NEVER guess the repo name — confirm with the user before `gh repo create`.
- If there are no changes to commit and the remote is already up to date, say so and stop.
- If a pre-commit hook fails, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (don't amend).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Obsidian MCP — books knowledge base

An MCP server over an Obsidian vault that holds a personal knowledge base of
math + CS books. It follows the "LLM knowledge base" pattern: raw source
material is ingested into `raw/`, and an LLM incrementally compiles a `wiki/`
of concept articles on top of it. The LLM reads and writes the markdown; you
read it in Obsidian.

The server talks to the vault through the
[coddingtonbear/obsidian-local-rest-api](https://github.com/coddingtonbear/obsidian-local-rest-api)
plugin (HTTPS + bearer token on `127.0.0.1:27124`).

## Architecture

Three top-level folders, by design — nothing else:

```
raw/                              # ingest layer — sources + your notes
  <book>/
    <book>.md                     # hub: frontmatter + nav callout + summary
    *.pdf                         # sources: textbook, solutions, errata, ...
    notes.md                      # notes INDEX (frontmatter `book: [[X]]`)
    notes/<slug>.md               # atomic notes (`parent: [[notes]]`)
  notes/                          # a "book" that isn't a book — meta notes
    notes.md
    notes/<slug>.md

attachments/<book>/*.png|jpg      # screenshots, diagrams

wiki/                             # compiled layer — LLM-written (see Roadmap)
```

### Notes model — atomic, Zettelkasten-style

- `notes.md` is an **index** node: minimal body, `book: [[X]]` in frontmatter.
- Each thought is an **atomic note** at `raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md` whose
  frontmatter is `parent: [[notes]]` only. The book is reached transitively
  (`note → notes → book`), so there's no direct note→book edge.
- Obsidian surfaces children as backlinks automatically — `notes.md` never has
  to list them.

### Graph node types

`raw_graph()` classifies every node by path shape:

| type           | path shape                          |
|----------------|-------------------------------------|
| `hub`          | `raw/<book>/<book>.md`              |
| `notes`        | `raw/<book>/notes.md`               |
| `note`         | `raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md`        |
| `index`        | `wiki/index.md`                     |
| `concept`      | `wiki/concepts/<slug>.md`           |
| `book-summary` | `wiki/books/<slug>.md`              |
| `pdf`          | `*.pdf`                             |
| `image`        | `*.png .jpg .jpeg .webp .svg .gif`  |
| `other_md`     | any other `.md`                     |
| `other`        | anything else                       |

# Obsidian MCP — books knowledge base

An MCP server over an Obsidian vault that holds a personal knowledge base of
math + CS books. It follows the "LLM knowledge base" pattern: raw source
material is ingested into `raw/`, and an LLM incrementally compiles a `wiki/`
of concept articles on top of it. The LLM reads and writes the markdown; you
read it in Obsidian.

The server talks to the vault through the
[coddingtonbear/obsidian-local-rest-api](https://github.com/coddingtonbear/obsidian-local-rest-api)
plugin (HTTPS + bearer token on `127.0.0.1:27124`).

## Architecture

Three top-level folders, by design — nothing else:

```
raw/                              # ingest layer — sources + your notes
  <book>/
    <book>.md                     # hub: frontmatter + nav callout + summary
    *.pdf                         # sources: textbook, solutions, errata, ...
    notes.md                      # notes INDEX (frontmatter `book: [[X]]`)
    notes/<slug>.md               # atomic notes (`parent: [[notes]]`)
  notes/                          # a "book" that isn't a book — meta notes
    notes.md
    notes/<slug>.md

attachments/<book>/*.png|jpg      # screenshots, diagrams

wiki/                             # compiled layer — LLM-written (see Roadmap)
```

### Notes model — atomic, Zettelkasten-style

- `notes.md` is an **index** node: minimal body, `book: [[X]]` in frontmatter.
- Each thought is an **atomic note** at `raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md` whose
  frontmatter is `parent: [[notes]]` only. The book is reached transitively
  (`note → notes → book`), so there's no direct note→book edge.
- Obsidian surfaces children as backlinks automatically — `notes.md` never has
  to list them.

|----------------|-------------------------------------|
| `hub`          | `raw/<book>/<book>.md`              |
| `notes`        | `raw/<book>/notes.md`               |
| `note`         | `raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md`        |
| `index`        | `wiki/index.md`                     |
| `concept`      | `wiki/concepts/<slug>.md`           |
| `book-summary` | `wiki/books/<slug>.md`              |

# compile-wiki

 Download the versioned AppImage, then symlink it to a stable filename (`Beeper.AppImage`). The systemd unit and all docs reference the symlink — when Beeper updates, you only retarget the symlink, no unit edit, no `daemon-reload`. Skipping the symlink leaves the unit hardcoded to a version that will eventually disappear and put `beeper.service` into an infinite `status=203/EXEC` restart loop.
 
+> **Auto-update safety net.** Beeper updates itself in the background by dropping a new `Beeper-<ver>-x86_64.AppImage` into `~/Downloads` and **deleting the old one** — without retargeting your symlink, so it silently goes dangling. The currently-running process keeps going (its files are mounted under `/tmp`), so nothing breaks until the next restart/reboot → `203/EXEC`. To make this impossible, the `beeper.service` in Step 7 has an `ExecStartPre` that repoints `Beeper.AppImage` at the newest versioned AppImage in `~/Downloads` on **every** start. You still create the symlink manually here so the very first launch (Step 3, before the service exists) works.
